behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily retry-policy math/state inside one boundary (failure count -> delay with cap), with no required OS/network/subprocess or model output. Rejected higher level: behavior text is only about delay progression/cap, not transport or cross-module handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Test the backoff calculator/state object directly: feed consecutive failure counts and assert delays are 2x each step until the configured max, then remain capped.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at a module boundary: response classification + retry policy handoff. A unit test of only parser or only backoff logic can miss wiring mistakes. No real HTTP is required; a stubbed response object is sufficient, so not System. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry classifier + backoff policy + call orchestrator with a fake transport returning a 429/rate-limit payload/headers; assert the retry path is chosen and computed wait matches policy/header precedence.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: This requires orchestration across timer/backoff + request execution + response return path. In-process fake clock and fake transport can reproduce the real failure, so System is not required. No AI/ML.
behaviors.test_description: Using fake timers and a scripted fake transport (first rate-limited, then success), assert the second attempt is sent with the original request payload and `call()` resolves to the success value without exposing retry internals.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in end-of-retry-chain error mapping (retry counter + terminal decision + typed error construction), which spans components. Real network/OS is unnecessary; deterministic fake responses can drive exhaustion. No AI/ML.
behaviors.test_description: Drive repeated rate-limit failures via fake transport until max retries is reached; assert rejection type is `QuotaExhaustedError` (and not generic error), with expected metadata such as attempts/wait context if defined.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Quoted escalation-relevant phrase: "full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking" indicates multi-module state progression, so Unit is too low. Rejected System: nothing in behavior requires real OS/network/subprocess; in-process fake transport + fake time can expose infinite-loop bugs. No AI/ML, so not Agentic/Workflow.
behaviors.test_description: Run orchestrator with fake timers and transport that always returns rate-limit; assert bounded number of attempts, monotonic/capped waits, and terminal error within expected virtual time/attempt limits (no unbounded loop).

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Quoted phrase "multiple async callers in the same process" and "same shared quota bucket" describes cross-caller shared-state coordination, a module interaction boundary. Rejected System: "in the same process" disqualifies need for real distributed/network infra; in-process concurrency test can catch race/state-split bugs. No AI/ML.
behaviors.test_description: Fire concurrent `call()` invocations against one client instance with controlled fake transport and deterministic scheduler; assert all callers consume from one quota counter/bucket and enforcement is globally consistent across callers.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Queue ordering under contention is an interaction between enqueueing, scheduler/release, and dispatch modules; unit-testing a comparator alone misses real handoff bugs. Rejected System: ordering can be validated with in-process async control/fake timers; no real OS/network primitive is required. No AI/ML.
behaviors.test_description: Enqueue multiple blocked requests with known submission order, then release capacity one slot at a time; assert dispatch/transport invocation order is FIFO and matches original submission sequence.
